EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic front brought prolonged frigid temperatures to western Nebraska Sunday, January 25 through the morning of Monday, January 26. The coldest temperatures occurred Sunday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures ranged from -5 F to -10 F across Banner County from 1/25 into 1/26. The combination of cold temperatures and gusty winds resulted in a prolonged period of extreme wind chills under -25 F. Minimum observed wind chill values reached -31 F at the Bravo AWOS (14 W Harrisburg) at 7:55 PM MST on 1/25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Summary of ice event in Nebraska.
A secondary arctic front dropping down from Canada lead to another round of brief high winds in the wind prones of southeast Wyoming and the northern Nebraska panhandle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front moved across the area during the evening hours of January 15th. Strong winds continued overnight and into the following day. Numerous power outages, damages, and road closures were reported with the abnormally strong northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 72 MPH was observed at the Kings Canyon sensor (7 S Chadron) at 11:22 AM MST on 1/16. Multiple sensors across the county recorded strong wind gusts over 60 MPH. Downed tree branches and power flashes were also reported in the city of Chadron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From late January 22 into January 23, 2026, an arctic front advanced through eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa, ushering a bitterly cold airmass into the region. Northerly wind gusts of 10 to 15 mph, combined with minimum temperatures of 5 to 12 degrees below zero, produced dangerous wind chill values of 30 to 35 degrees below zero across northeast Nebraska and western Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No observations within the county. Estimated minimum wind chill temperatures of 30 to 35 degrees below zero based on surrounding observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 16, 2026, gusty winds and snow squalls impacted eastern Nebraska. A shortwave trough pivoting across the Northern Plains drove an initial cold front and strong cold air advection (CAA) into the region. Behind a secondary cold front, enhanced atmospheric mixing tapped into higher winds aloft, transferring them to the surface. While widespread peak gusts ranged from 45 to 55 mph, isolated locations briefly recorded gusts of 58 to 62 mph. Additionally, horizontal convective rolls trailing the secondary front generated transient heavy snow showers, resulting in two distinct snow squalls. The intense winds within these bands caused localized blowing snow and rapidly reduced visibilities to 1/4 mile or less. The first squall moved through Cuming County during the late morning, followed by a second in Lancaster County during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall conditions impacted the county between 10:43 AM and 11:06 AM CST. KOAX radar indicated a robust convective band with high reflectivity moving rapidly through the area. During the passage of this band, various NDOT road cameras displayed near-zero visibility (under 1/4 mile).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through southwest Nebraska on the evening of the 15th. Multiple wind gusts ranging from 55 mph - 63 mph were recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KMCK reported wind gusts of 61 and 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through southwest Nebraska on the evening of the 15th. Multiple wind gusts ranging from 55 mph - 63 mph were recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station at 1 WNW Culbertson recorded wind gust of 57 and 58 mph. A personal weather station in Trenton recorded a wind gust of 56 mph. A dead tree was uprooted in Trenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 21, 2026, a shortwave disturbance moving across the northern Plains drove a cold front through eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa. Ahead of the frontal passage, northwesterly winds strengthened throughout the afternoon, driven by a tightening surface pressure gradient and deep mixing that tapped into stronger winds aloft. Consequently, strong, non-thunderstorm wind gusts overspread the region, with measured peak gusts ranging from 58 to 71 mph. Notable observations included 71 mph in Tekamah, NE, 64 mph near Verdel, NE, and 61 mph in Harlan, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station measured wind gust of 64 mph near Verdel, 60 mph near Creighton, and 58 mph near Bloomfield, NE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 21, 2026, a shortwave disturbance moving across the northern Plains drove a cold front through eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa. Ahead of the frontal passage, northwesterly winds strengthened throughout the afternoon, driven by a tightening surface pressure gradient and deep mixing that tapped into stronger winds aloft. Consequently, strong, non-thunderstorm wind gusts overspread the region, with measured peak gusts ranging from 58 to 71 mph. Notable observations included 71 mph in Tekamah, NE, 64 mph near Verdel, NE, and 61 mph in Harlan, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station measured wind gust of 60 mph near Kennard, NE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From late January 22 into January 23, 2026, an arctic front advanced through eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa, ushering a bitterly cold airmass into the region. Northerly wind gusts of 10 to 15 mph, combined with minimum temperatures of 5 to 12 degrees below zero, produced dangerous wind chill values of 30 to 35 degrees below zero across northeast Nebraska and western Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No observations within the county. Estimated minimum wind chill temperatures of 30 to 39 degrees below zero based on surrounding observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds behind yet another arctic front reached speeds over 40 mph with gusts as high as 60 across northeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained at 40 to 46 mph near Emerson and Ponca.  Winds removed several shingles from a home in Concord.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds behind yet another arctic front reached speeds over 40 mph with gusts as high as 60 across northeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained at 40 to 46 mph near Emerson and gusted as high as 59 mph at South Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds surged behind a strong arctic cold front which moved across the region early on January 16. The strongest winds were generally near the Missouri River Valley where gusts to 50 to 55 mph were common. While areas of light snowfall generally on the order of a dusting to an inch fell before and concurrent with the strong winds, there were only very spotty and brief instances of visibility in blowing and falling snow below one mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 30 to 32 mph at times with a peak gust of 49 mph at a Nebraska Road Weather Information System site 1 mile north of Emerson.
